Builder · Hands-On Field Work

Ryan Burchill

rburchill17@gmail.com (603) 957-0720 New York, NY
Available NYC — Sept 2026 Scroll to see the builds

A few years into engineering, I found desk-bound work wasn't where I did my best thinking. Over the past three years bartending in Boston, I became the go-to facilities and build lead for a five-location restaurant group — assembling and installing walk-in refrigerators and freezers from panel layout through compressor hookup and leak testing, building out a new location from rough-in to opening day, and keeping draft, refrigeration, and kitchen equipment running across sites.

The bar work sharpened what the technical roles never tested: reading a room and keeping composure when things go sideways. I'm relocating to NYC in September 2026 for field roles where engineering knowledge and hands-on work both count. The two builds below are the proof.

Project 01 — Ground-Up Build

Two-Story Barn Garage

Built from the foundation to the finished roof with my father, a master carpenter — the two of us the entire crew.

Working alongside my father, a master carpenter, I built a two-story 25′×50′ barn garage from the ground up to solve a chronic space shortage for the family's vehicles and his tool inventory — on a tight budget. The two of us were the entire crew, handling every phase from foundation to finished roof over roughly eight months.

Title Block
Project
Two-story barn garage, ground-up
Size
25′ × 50′, two stories
Timeline
~8 months, foundation to roof
Crew
Two-person (with father, master carpenter)
Role
Co-builder across every phase
Scope
Foundation · framing · siding · roofing

What the build involved

  • Set the rebar lattice and poured the concrete foundation.
  • Framed the wall structure; handled layout and dimensioning with laser distance meter and chalk line.
  • Installed and stained cedar shingle siding.
  • Framed and shingled the roof.

What it demonstrates

  • Fluency across hand and power tools: table/circular saw, recip/saber saw, drills, laser measure, tape, chalk line.
  • Precise layout and a hands-on feel for how structural components fit and carry load.
  • Reliability and stamina across a multi-month project, start to finish.
  • Coordinating directly under a master tradesperson — the mentor-led dynamic of an apprenticeship.
Project 02 — Design + Build

Vocal Isolation Booth

Designed and built solo — a layered wall system engineered to keep outside noise out of the recording space.

I designed and built a vocal isolation booth for my studio, engineered to keep external noise from bleeding into the recording space. The walls are a layered composite — wood framing, two-inch construction foam, and sound-deadening blanket fabric — with every panel cut and fitted to tight tolerances to close the air gaps and seams that cause acoustic bleed.

Title Block
Project
Vocal isolation booth, design + build
Role
Sole designer and builder
System
Layered wall: wood frame + 2″ foam + sound blanket
Goal
Block exterior noise bleed into recording space
Scope
Design · cut/fit · fasten · finish

What the build involved

  • Designed the layered wall assembly as a single sealed system.
  • Cut and fitted each panel to tight tolerances to minimize gaps and seam leakage.
  • Framed and fastened the enclosure with nails, screws, and staples across structural and finish layers.
  • Sanded and finished components for clean, tightly sealed joints.

What it demonstrates

  • Engineering design applied to a physical build: set a performance goal, design the assembly to meet it.
  • Precision fit and tolerance control across dissimilar materials — rigid wood, compressible foam, flexible fabric.
  • Fluency across cutting, fastening, and finishing tools: table/circular/saber saw, orbital + rotary sanders, screw gun, staple gun, hammer, tape, chalk line.
  • A practical grasp of eliminating leak paths — the same sealing instinct that carries to building envelopes, equipment enclosures, and mechanical fit-up.
